bins.name: NERC BPS authority scope
bins.desc: NERC supports Commission action on large loads but insists any resulting order reference the full Bulk Power System, not just the Bulk Electric System subset. NERC declines to weigh in on FERC's jurisdiction under sections other than 215 and notes that large load users are potential BPS users under existing precedent. The stance is mixed because NERC supports the proceeding while also correcting what it sees as a jurisdictional misdescription in the ANOPR.
quotes: NERC requests that any ensuing Commission action accurately reflects the scope of NERC's reliability authority, which statutorily extends to Reliability Standards that provide for the reliable operation of the BPS and is distinguished from the Commission's jurisdiction regarding the transmission and sale of electric energy.

NERC declines to comment on matters outside its purview, such as the proposed ANOPR assertions regarding the Commission's jurisdiction under other provisions of the Federal Power Act, including section 205.
Among the Secretary's proposed principles is a proposal that 'utilities serving large loads must meet all applicable NERC reliability standards….'

there is opportunity to further clarify NERC's authority as the ERO under section 215, because one of the proposed ANOPR principles suggest that NERC's registration categories and Reliability Standards are to ensure the 'reliability of the BES.'
NERC requests out of an abundance of caution that any subsequent Commission action in this proceeding reflects the full scope of NERC's reliability authority as pertaining to the BPS.

NERC currently has limited registry criteria applicable to 'users' of the BPS. Nevertheless, FERC precedent and legislative history of the Federal Power Act indicate that users of the BPS mean all those who 'use' the BPS.

FERC stated its concerns that, '[under NERC's proposed definition] a large industrial customer that receives electric energy directly from the [BPS] may not be defined as a user of the BPS, even though it may directly affect the reliability of the [BPS].'

Although NERC has not developed any criteria that would add large load entities to the NCR at this time, NERC requests that any resulting Commission action reflect NERC's scope of statutory authority as the ERO to ensure the reliability of the BPS.

bins.name: Reliability study and standards review
bins.desc: NERC supports studying large load impacts on the BPS and is already conducting that work through the Large Loads Action Plan, white papers, a Level 2 Alert, and a draft Reliability Guideline. NERC expects to deliver findings and next steps to FERC by Q1 2026, including analysis of whether registry criteria or Reliability Standards revisions are warranted.
quotes: Such unique challenges include demand forecasting and planning for system behavior when some large loads, such as data centers for cryptocurrency mining and artificial intelligence, are substantially larger and constructed more quickly than prior large loads using the BPS.

NERC plans to coordinate with stakeholders over the following year to explore potential revisions to the registry criteria and Reliability Standards that would incorporate large loads impacting the reliable operation of the BPS.

the Large Loads Action Plan includes an action item on registration analysis, which analyzes the legal basis for registration of large users of the BPS and whether there are Reliability Standards requirements that could apply to such users.
In the first quarter of 2026, NERC will develop additional findings, guidance, and next steps to account for the BPS reliability needs associated with large loads.
By February 27, 2026, NERC will submit a report to the Commission regarding responses to the Level 2 Alert.

bins.name: NERC section 215 authority
bins.desc: NERC explains at length that its authority under Federal Power Act section 215 covers the Bulk Power System and all its users, owners, and operators, but does not extend to local distribution or to compelling construction of new capacity. NERC requests that Commission language reflect this statutory scope rather than narrowing it to the BES.
quotes: NERC requests that any ensuing Commission action accurately reflects the scope of NERC's reliability authority, which statutorily extends to Reliability Standards that provide for the reliable operation of the BPS and is distinguished from the Commission's jurisdiction regarding the transmission and sale of electric energy.

NERC declines to comment on matters outside its purview, such as the proposed ANOPR assertions regarding the Commission's jurisdiction under other provisions of the Federal Power Act, including section 205.
Among the Secretary's proposed principles is a proposal that 'utilities serving large loads must meet all applicable NERC reliability standards….'

there is opportunity to further clarify NERC's authority as the ERO under section 215, because one of the proposed ANOPR principles suggest that NERC's registration categories and Reliability Standards are to ensure the 'reliability of the BES.'
NERC requests out of an abundance of caution that any subsequent Commission action in this proceeding reflects the full scope of NERC's reliability authority as pertaining to the BPS.

bins.name: Registering large loads with NERC
bins.desc: NERC acknowledges it has no current registry criteria covering large load customers but explains that statutory and regulatory authority to register them as BPS users exists. NERC is conducting legal and technical analysis through its action plan and will engage stakeholders before proposing any criteria revisions.
quotes: NERC currently has limited registry criteria applicable to 'users' of the BPS. Nevertheless, FERC precedent and legislative history of the Federal Power Act indicate that users of the BPS mean all those who 'use' the BPS.

FERC stated its concerns that, '[under NERC's proposed definition] a large industrial customer that receives electric energy directly from the [BPS] may not be defined as a user of the BPS, even though it may directly affect the reliability of the [BPS].'

Although NERC has not developed any criteria that would add large load entities to the NCR at this time, NERC requests that any resulting Commission action reflect NERC's scope of statutory authority as the ERO to ensure the reliability of the BPS.

NERC plans to coordinate with stakeholders over the following year to explore potential revisions to the registry criteria and Reliability Standards that would incorporate large loads impacting the reliable operation of the BPS.

the Large Loads Action Plan includes an action item on registration analysis, which analyzes the legal basis for registration of large users of the BPS and whether there are Reliability Standards requirements that could apply to such users.

bins.name: Unprecedented large load demand growth
bins.desc: NERC's own assessments confirm that demand growth is at a two-decade high and that data centers for AI and cryptocurrency mining are larger and built faster than prior loads. NERC treats this as an urgent reliability challenge requiring prompt action on its own timeline.
quotes: demand growth is higher than at any point in the last two decades.

Such unique challenges include demand forecasting and planning for system behavior when some large loads, such as data centers for cryptocurrency mining and artificial intelligence, are substantially larger and constructed more quickly than prior large loads using the BPS.
In the first quarter of 2026, NERC will develop additional findings, guidance, and next steps to account for the BPS reliability needs associated with large loads.
By February 27, 2026, NERC will submit a report to the Commission regarding responses to the Level 2 Alert.
